sentences_input.text: Now the reason I somewhat shifted into this field was because I was trying to explain that members of the family in our town-- the-- so if I go back, historically speaking, the family, according to the information I had at home-- was that while the family originated from Bohemia, as you probably know-- it's written in many history books, for those who care to look for it, that in Bohemia and Moravia, which is today the Czech Republic, was one of the most learned and well-to-do Jewish communities in Europe in the times of Maria Theresa, which was in the 1700s.

Except that the community got decimated by the anti-Jewish edicts given out by Maria Theresa, who was a singularly capable Jew hater, to the extent that there is an anecdote that she swore to herself never to set eye on a Jew.
And when, in one of her wars, she needed some extraordinary sum of money, she nevertheless called [INAUDIBLE] from Frankfurt.
And she erected a sheet of linen.
Like in the Orthodox weddings these days, they do a mechitza.
And while she took his money-- or the government, it was accepted to take loans, she refused to ever look at him.
Nevertheless, this was presumably acceptable to Jews those days.

And the-- the point I was trying to make is that the community got decimated by one of the most serious anti-Jewish regulations, which were voting to force that in every Jewish family, only one child who got married got permission of residence, whether it was in Prague, Brunn, or whatever place in the Czech Republic, Bohemia and Moravia.
And anybody else, any other children could stay there, provided they were not married.
